intro_to_table_design.label: Intro to Table Design
intro_to_table_design.summary: Learn what `CREATE TABLE` does, how to choose clear column names, how to pick useful data types, and how to design tables so they are easy to understand later.

sketch1.title: How do you choose column names?

sketch2.title: How do you choose data types?

sketch3.title: How do you design for clarity?

what_create_table_does.title: `CREATE TABLE` defines a new table structure.
what_create_table_does.bodyMarkdown: Until now, many beginner queries worked with tables that already existed.¥n¥n`CREATE TABLE` is different.¥nIt is used to define:¥n¥n- the table name¥n- the columns¥n- the data types¥n- sometimes the rules for the data too¥n¥nSo the big idea is:¥n¥n> `CREATE TABLE` is how you design a table before putting data into it

choosing_column_names.title: Good column names should be clear and easy to understand.
choosing_column_names.bodyMarkdown: A strong column name should tell the reader what the data means.¥n¥nBeginner-friendly names are usually:¥n¥n- specific¥n- simple¥n- consistent¥n¥nFor example, `student_name` is usually clearer than something vague like `info1`.¥n¥nSo the key idea is:¥n¥n> good column names make the table easier to read and use later

choosing_data_types.title: Each column should use a data type that fits the kind of value it stores.
choosing_data_types.bodyMarkdown: A table works best when each column is designed for the right kind of data.¥n¥nFor example:¥n¥n- ids often use integers¥n- names often use text¥n- prices often use decimal-style numbers¥n- dates are often stored as date-like text in beginner SQLite work¥n¥nSo the main idea is:¥n¥n> the data type should match the kind of information in the column

designing_for_clarity.title: A clear table design is easier to learn, query, and check in the SQL workspace.
designing_for_clarity.bodyMarkdown: Good table design is not only about being correct.¥nIt is also about being readable.¥n¥nIn this module, after you run `CREATE TABLE`, you can use:¥n¥n- the **Tables** tab to inspect the table you created¥n- later, the **ERD** tab to see relationships¥n- later, the **Chen** tab to see entities and attributes¥n¥nSo the important idea is:¥n¥n> clear design helps both SQL and humans understand the table

m13_s01_create_students_shell.title: Create a simple students table
m13_s01_create_students_shell.prompt: The SQLite workspace is using the blank `ddl_blank` dataset.¥n¥nWrite a query that creates a table named `students` with these columns:¥n¥n- `id` as `INTEGER`¥n- `name` as `TEXT`¥n- `grade_level` as `INTEGER`¥n- `city` as `TEXT`¥n¥nThen click **Run**.¥nAfter that, open the **Tables** tab and inspect the table structure.

help.concept: A `CREATE TABLE` query defines the table name and each column's name and type.
help.hint_1: Start with `CREATE TABLE students (` and then list each column on its own line.
help.hint_2: Build the table by naming it first, then writing each column with its type inside parentheses, separated by commas.

m13_s01_create_students_shell.hint: Write `CREATE TABLE`, then list each column name with its type.

m13_s01_create_products_shell.title: Create a simple products table
m13_s01_create_products_shell.prompt: The dataset is blank.¥n¥nWrite a query that creates a table named `products` with these columns:¥n¥n- `id` as `INTEGER`¥n- `name` as `TEXT`¥n- `price` as `REAL`¥n- `category` as `TEXT`¥n¥nThen run it and inspect the **Tables** tab.

help.concept: A table definition becomes clearer when the column names describe the kind of values they will store.
help.hint_1: Use `CREATE TABLE products (...)` and list the four columns inside.
help.hint_2: Make sure each column uses the correct name and the matching type before you run the statement.

m13_s01_create_products_shell.hint: Name the table first, then define the columns inside parentheses.

m13_s01_create_events_shell.title: Create a table with date-like columns
m13_s01_create_events_shell.prompt: The dataset is blank.¥n¥nWrite a query that creates a table named `events` with these columns:¥n¥n- `id` as `INTEGER`¥n- `title` as `TEXT`¥n- `event_date` as `TEXT`¥n- `created_at` as `TEXT`¥n¥nThen click **Run**.

help.concept: In beginner SQLite work, date-like values are often stored as text columns.
help.hint_1: Use `TEXT` for both date-like columns here.
help.hint_2: Build the table normally, and give the date-style columns names that clearly describe what they mean.

m13_s01_create_events_shell.hint: Use `TEXT` for the date-style columns in this beginner table.

m13_s01_create_clear_contacts_table.title: Design for clarity
m13_s01_create_clear_contacts_table.prompt: The dataset is blank.¥n¥nWrite a query that creates a table named `contacts` with clear column names:¥n¥n- `id` as `INTEGER`¥n- `full_name` as `TEXT`¥n- `email` as `TEXT`¥n- `phone_number` as `TEXT`¥n¥nThen run it.

help.concept: Good table design uses column names that clearly describe the stored meaning.
help.hint_1: Use the exact column names listed in the prompt.
help.hint_2: The main goal is clarity: choose the table name and column names exactly as given, then assign the matching types.

m13_s01_create_clear_contacts_table.hint: Use the clearer names exactly as listed.
m13_s01_create_clear_contacts_table.starterCode: -- Create a contacts table with clear column names¥n

m13_s01_intro_design_project.title: Practice basic table design
m13_s01_intro_design_project.prompt: Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice `CREATE TABLE` on the blank `ddl_blank` dataset.¥n¥nFor each task:¥n¥n1. write the table definition carefully¥n2. click **Run**¥n3. open the **Tables** tab to inspect the table you created¥n4. check whether the column names and types feel clear¥n¥nTasks:¥n¥n1. Create a `students` table¥n2. Create a `products` table¥n3. Create an `events` table with date-like text columns¥n4. Create a `contacts` table with clear names¥n5. Create a `books` table with `id`, `title`, `author_name`, and `published_at`¥n¥nRun one query at a time.¥nThis project is about learning the basic shape of table design.

help.concept: Beginner table design starts with four things: a clear table name, clear column names, fitting data types, and readable structure.
help.hint_1: Keep the table names simple and the columns meaningful.
help.hint_2: Solve the tasks one by one. Focus first on what the table is for, then name the columns clearly, then choose types that fit the values.

m13_s01_intro_design_project.hint: Start with what the table is for, then name and type the columns clearly.

m13_s01_create_table_meaning.title: What does CREATE TABLE do?
m13_s01_create_table_meaning.prompt: What is the main job of `CREATE TABLE` in SQL?

help.concept: `CREATE TABLE` defines a new table and its structure.
help.hint_2: It is used before inserting data, because it defines the table layout first.

m13_s01_create_table_meaning.hint: Think define a new table structure.

options.a: It defines a new table and its structure
options.b: It only reads rows from a table

m13_s01_column_names_meaning.title: Why do column names matter?
m13_s01_column_names_meaning.prompt: Why are clear column names important in beginner table design?

help.concept: Clear names make the table easier to understand later.
help.hint_2: A column name should help the reader understand the stored meaning quickly.

m13_s01_column_names_meaning.hint: Think clearer names make the table easier to read.

options.a: They make the table easier to understand later
options.b: They remove the need for data types

m13_s01_data_types_meaning.title: Why choose data types carefully?
m13_s01_data_types_meaning.prompt: Why is choosing the right data type important when designing a table?

help.concept: The type should match the kind of value the column will store.
help.hint_2: A column works best when its type matches the kind of data it is meant to hold.

m13_s01_data_types_meaning.hint: Think the type should fit the kind of value.

options.a: Because the type should match the kind of value stored in the column
options.b: Because every column should always use TEXT
options.c: Because data types only matter in SELECT

m13_s01_clarity_meaning.title: What does designing for clarity mean?
m13_s01_clarity_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of designing a table for clarity?

help.concept: A clear table is easier to read, use, and check later.
help.hint_2: Clarity means the names, types, and layout all make sense together.

m13_s01_clarity_meaning.hint: Think easy to read and easy to understand.

options.a: Make the table easy to read and understand later
options.d: Never inspect the table after creating it

m13_s01_tools_meaning.title: Why is the Tables tab useful here?
m13_s01_tools_meaning.prompt: Why is the **Tables** tab useful in Module 13 after running `CREATE TABLE`?

help.concept: The Tables tab helps learners inspect the structure they just created.
help.hint_2: It is a quick way to inspect the table name and the columns after the DDL query runs.

options.a: It lets you inspect the table you just created
options.c: It deletes the table after creation

m13_s01_create_table_true.title: What is true about CREATE TABLE?
m13_s01_create_table_true.prompt: Select all true statements about `CREATE TABLE`.

help.concept: `CREATE TABLE` is used to define a table name, columns, and types.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about designing the table before data is stored.

m13_s01_create_table_true.hint: Think table definition, not row reading.

options.e: `CREATE TABLE` is part of database design

m13_s01_column_names_true.title: What is true about choosing column names?
m13_s01_column_names_true.prompt: Select all true statements about choosing column names in beginner table design.

help.concept: Good column names make the table easier to understand and use.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about names that help readers understand the data.

m13_s01_column_names_true.hint: Think clear names that describe the data.

options.a: Good column names should describe the stored meaning
options.b: Clear names make later queries easier to read
options.d: Column names should be vague whenever possible
options.e: Readable names help the table design feel cleaner

m13_s01_data_types_true.title: What is true about choosing data types?
m13_s01_data_types_true.prompt: Select all true statements about choosing data types in beginner table design.

help.concept: The chosen type should fit the intended kind of value in the column.
help.hint_1: Think fit the type to the meaning.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about matching types to the stored values.

m13_s01_data_types_true.hint: Think the type should fit the data.

options.c: Prices often use decimal-style numeric types
options.d: All columns should always use the same type
options.e: Choosing better types improves the table design

m13_s01_clarity_true.title: What is true about designing for clarity?
m13_s01_clarity_true.prompt: Select all true statements about designing tables for clarity.

help.concept: Clear design helps learners understand and inspect the table more easily.
help.hint_1: Think readable names, readable types, readable structure.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about making the design easier for humans to understand.

options.a: Clarity makes a table easier to inspect later
options.b: Clarity makes later queries easier to understand
options.c: Clarity comes from good names and fitting types
options.d: Clarity means the design should be as confusing as possible
options.e: A clear design helps both learning and debugging

m13_s01_design_workflow_true.title: What matches a good beginner workflow for table design?
m13_s01_design_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good beginner workflow for designing a new table.

help.concept: A good workflow is to decide the table purpose, then name columns clearly, then choose fitting data types, then inspect the result.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that help turn an idea into a readable table definition.

m13_s01_design_workflow_true.hint: Start with the table purpose, then choose names and types carefully.

options.a: Decide what the table is for
options.d: Run the statement and inspect the table afterward
options.e: Ignore whether the column names make sense
